deck_pages.title: Exec Summary
deck_pages.text:
Exec Summary
Full Year 2025 Results
● Adj. EBITDA $10.8M, up $3.6M vs 12.19.25 Forecast of $7.2M (50% beat) driven by slightly higher revenue
attainment and strong expense control
○ YoY Company improved Adj. EBITDA $25M and Unlevered FCF improved $13M over 2024
● Company reached positive Adj. EBITDA and Unlevered Free Cash in 2025
2026 Floor Plan
● Projecting: Rev: $199M (-8% YoY) | 71% Gross Margin (-2.7% YoY) | $10M Adj. EBITDA (-6% YoY)
● Projecting Adj. EBITDA slightly down YoY due to declining revenue offset by expense control
Cash + Oxford Covenants
● Cash
○ $10M additional Oxford line drawn on 12/8/25
○ Cash $1.4M higher than forecast due to Adj. EBITDA beat
● Oxford Covenants
○ Stronger liquidity covenant protection vs last update due to higher cash balance via 2025 EBITDA beat
For the First Time in its History, Company Achieved Positive Adj. EBITDA and Unlevered Free Cash Flow in 2025

deck_pages.title: Full Year 2025 Financials
deck_pages.text:
Full Year 2025 Financials
Versus 12.19 Forecast
Bookings (-$1.5M):
Unfavorability primarily due to B2B Employer, Baker Tilly and Veolia
expansion push to Q1 26 (-$0.9M) and Betterfly partnership churn
(-$0.2M). B2B Health Plans decrease due to Virgin Pulse renewal timing
push to Q1 26 and lower clinical sessions ($0.5M).
Revenue (+$1.1M):
Favorability primarily due to B2B Employer one-time revenue from
Betterfly and Adobe partnerships, US Navy expansion, and higher lives
in some employee populations.
Gross Margin (+$2.3M):
Favorability primarily due to higher revenue and lower clinical demand
resulting in lower Care Provider expense.
Operational Expense (+$1.4M):
Favorability primarily due to lower professional fees, lower headcount,
lower healthcare expense, lower severance costs, and lower bonus
(95% attainment), partially oﬀset by lower capitalized R&D.
EBITDA (+$3.6M):
Favorability due to higher revenue and lower expenses.
Cash (+$1.4M):
EBITDA favorability of $3.6M oﬀset by lower net working capital due to
earlier AP payments.

Projected EBITDA is flat YoY mainly driven by lower revenue oﬀset by streamlined costs.
2026 vs. 2025
Bookings (-$25.0M):
D2C decrease primarily due to market conditions and 50% reduction in performance
marketing spend (-$18.5M). B2B Employer decrease due to potential higher churn
(-$4.9M). B2B Health Plans decline primarily due to recognition of Cigna implementation
bookings in 2025 (-$1.6M).
Revenue (-$17.2M):
Decline in D2C due to reasons mentioned above for bookings (-$14.5M). B2B Employer
decline due to increased churn noted above (-$3.9M). Higher B2B Health Plans due to new
Cigna sub-clinical (+$1.1M).
Gross Margin (-270bps):
Change in revenue mix. Less high margin D2C and B2B content revenue, increased
lower-margin EAP and D2C therapy revenue.
Operational Expense (+$17.3M):
Primary drivers of YoY expense reductions are ($10.8M) lower Performance Marketing and
($4.6M) lower salary & related due to the recent reorganization, and reduced professional
fees ($2.1M).
EBITDA (-$0.6M):
Primarily due to lower revenue and lower gross margin, mostly oﬀset by lower operating
expenses.
Cash (-$11.0M):
Declines primarily due to net interest expense ($9.2M) - see cash bridge slide for further
detail

deck_pages.title: 2026 Cash Bridge
deck_pages.text:
2026 Cash Bridge
Cash Flow Drivers Commentary
Adjusted EBITDA (+$10.2M)
- See slide 5 for detail
Interest Expense (Net) (-$9.2M)
- Primarily reflects debt service on Oxford facility
Net Working Capital (-$4.6M)
- Primarily year-over-year revenue decline, as most
customers pay in advance and lower sales reduce deferred
revenue and cash
Taxes & Other Adjustments (-$1.2M)
- Primarily sales, VAT , and corporate tax payments
Cash Flow from Investing (-$5.5M)
- Capitalized R&D spend. Higher in 2026 reflects more
investment.
Cash Flow from Financing (-$0.7M)
- Minimum required Oxford debt paydowns in 2026
- 2025 included $14.8M of net borrowings vs $0.7M net
paydown in 2026
Cash Flow down due to lower EBITDA, higher interest, and tax payment, oﬀset by $10M DDTL2 Draw on 12/8/25

deck_pages.title: FY26 Operating Plan
deck_pages.text: FY26 Operating Plan

deck_pages.title: Mental Health Market continues to evolve
deck_pages.text:
Mental Health Market continues to evolve
● Costs are climbing, driven by over-reliance on 1:1 therapy
● Therapy is often used as a first-line intervention, even for lower-acuity needs
● Employers are seeking more scalable, cost-effective entry points into care
● Burnout, stress, loneliness, and sleep issues are widespread
● These challenges drive productivity loss and downstream clinical cost
● Benefits leaders are prioritizing upstream prevention over reactive care
● Shift from “more sessions” to “right care, right time”
● Growing demand for stratified care and intelligent navigation
● Fewer vendors, better coordination across levels of support
1. Behavioral Health Costs
Continue to Rise
2. Subclinical Mental Health Is the
Largest Unmet Need
3. Buyers Want Orchestration,
not Just Access
● Engagement alone is no longer sufficient
● Employers increasingly focus on measurable improvement
● Faster recovery translates into lower cost and higher ROI
4. Outcomes and Speed of
Improvement Matter More
● Strong curiosity about AI-enabled care models
● Heightened concern around safety, privacy, and efficacy
● Preference for clinically credible, responsible AI leadership
5. AI Interest Is High, but Trust
Is the Gate

deck_pages.title: Taken together, these shifts favor integrated, stratified,
deck_pages.text:
Taken together, these shifts favor integrated, stratified,
outcomes-driven platforms that intervene earlier, guide
members intelligently, and responsibly use AI to improve
recovery speed and cost efficiency.
Mental Health Market continues to evolve

500k members enrolled

40% 3 mo MAU

60% mbrs partial symptom reduction
Top 5 priorities we need to nail this year. KRs should be highlighted at the top of WBR as the most important measures of success.

Placeholder. Will present latest stats

deck_pages.title: GTM Q4 2025 and YTD 2026 Highlights / Lowlights
deck_pages.text:
GTM Q4 2025 and YTD 2026 Highlights / Lowlights
Highlights
1. Q4 2025 Renewals closed at nearly 100% of July Forecast
($90.4m achieved versus $90.5m)
2. Amazon subclinical deal won, with May 1, 2026 launch
○ Covers 1.9 million lives with Core & Ebb (global) and
Coaching, Assessments, Triage (US only)
○ Utilization-based deal, projected to yield $6-9m year 1
○ Validates commercial viability of subclinical product
3. Cigna Evernorth expansion won, for 2026 in-year launch
○ 1.6m additional lives above original 7.5m Q4 deal
4. Renpho brand & content licensing deal closed
○ Embeds Headspace into eye massager physical device
○ Projected to yield $1m per year from 3 components:
■ $100K upfront licensing revenue
■ Per unit sales royalty
■ Revenue share for Headspace app upsells
Lowlights
1. Existing deal economics
2. New deal launch challenges
3. Sales team attrition

deck_pages.title: Health Plan 2026 YTD Summary
deck_pages.text:
Health Plan 2026 YTD Summary
Payer Pipeline
Build
Kaiser
Expansion
Cigna
Evernorth
Behavioral Care
(EBCG)
Initiated discussions with BH leaders at 10 health plans,
including 4 national/ multi-state payers, 3 Blues plans,
and 3 regional plans. Nearly all payers wanted follow-up
meetings.
Talking with national behavioral health leadership team
on three initiatives:
● Direct member steerage to coaching
● Website redesign
● Single-session coaching
Engagement with Cigna captive provider group:
● Headspace referrals to EBCG
● EBCG clinicians prescribing our content
● Assessments facilitation
● Referrals from EBCG to Headspace
● Benefit expense problem statement is
resonating; outpatient therapy spend is major
concern, plans looking for help
● Other BH vendors address this issue, with less
comprehensive of a solution
● Steerage can drive meaningful in-year coaching
utilization (+10-20%)
● Kaiser website redesign can increase content
app utilization (effect TBD)
● Our content, navigation, and coaching complement
clinical therapy, driving differentiation for therapists
● Multiple therapist networks have made inbound
inquiries on Headspace as an adjunct to their care
● Partnering with EBCG is especially attractive, as
our Cigna contract has built-in monetization (~90%
of EBCG patients are covered by Cigna insurance)
Initiative Key Updates Lessons Learned
Health Plans

deck_pages.title: Employer 2026 YTD Summary
deck_pages.text:
Employer 2026 YTD Summary
Employer
Consultant
Relations
EAP Strategy
Redefinition
Pipeline building activities in 2026 revolve around a
Consultant Relationship Management function three
times the size of the 2025 team. This enables
significantly greater education and coverage of
consultant relationships.
Pivoted former Mercer partner, Sandra Kuhn, to drive
EAP growth strategy, including:
● Value proposition definition
● Headspace positioning versus competitors
● Operational integration with CCA
● Employer EAP pipeline opportunities are largely
driven by broker relationships
● Broker activity is driven regionally with key
relationships that require cultivation
● We will have greater presence at regional broker
events & office locations
● Market signals indicate EAP market is moving
towards mid-priced EAP solutions
● Middle market lacks definition of trade-offs and
expectations for reduced price points
● Opportunities exist in industry verticals to develop
special programs and moats
Initiative Key Updates Lessons Learned
Employers
CCA
Renegotiation
We are negotiating a potential contract extension with our
EAP partner CCA, including:
● Stronger operational integration
● Improved margin-enhancing economics
● CCA is seeking a 3-4 year extension (current
contract expires in April, 2027
● Price reductions will enable Headspace to
participate in scale economies from dramatic
growth in our EAP business

deck_pages.title: Early EAP Strategic Principles
deck_pages.text:
Early EAP Strategic Principles
Employers
Current State Near-Term Future State
Feature-rich EAP model with capabilities and
experiences siloed between Headspace & subcontractor
EAP as orchestrated front door for workforce mental
health and employee support
Integration gaps with work-life, crisis management, and
in-person care subcontractor create experience gaps and
diminished member value
Seamless navigation across content, coaching, clinical
care, and EAP services
Generic self-guided content and early engagement
campaigns
Industry-specific programs built to maximize population
engagement
Utilization-based metrics between discrete Headspace
and CCA services
Outcomes-based value measured across the full
combined spectrum of services
Unclear differentiation among employers and consultants
on capabilities / features between EAP vendors
Clear positioning in market on Headspace services and
differentiated value proposition

deck_pages.title: Engagement Trends
deck_pages.text:
Engagement Trends
Product
● January MAU reflects an increase in member count (Members +2% MoM)
but even larger gains in engagement rates (MAU +12%).
● This increase includes growing engagement with Ebb (+37% MAU since
June). Launch of daily fresh experience on HS, with Timely Topics in Jan.
● January is typically a highly engaged month. However, Jan ʻ26 MAU is -13%
YoY, suggesting a greater need for new product innovations.
Notes: (1) MAU includes all users in a free or paid state who have had content, coaching, clinical, psychiatry, therapy, and/or Ebb engagement during the reported month. (2) MAU
does not include up to ~7500 Sondermind and CCA members pending new data ingestion, Includes members mapped to B2B or DTC relationships. (3) Ebb: Default is text so that
category includes “abandoners” (anyone exploring, opening Ebb and closing it immediately or after 1 message)
● Strong pattern of deeper interaction using Voice, both in total
duration and the number of member messages sent.
● There is a long tail of very long interactions with the 95th percentile
around 30 mins for both voice and text.
● Release on Feb 9 will let members set Voice as default. The speech to
text system can sometimes break up input into chunks inflating
messages, we will investigate events to make sure they are
instrumented properly.

Headspace Innovation Roadmap 2026
1 2
AI as a Care
Multiplier

and quality without
replacing human-care.
3

Experiences

across wellness, coaching, therapy,
and psychiatry. Introduce journeys
tailored to wellness tracking, and
Tiered oﬀering.
Expand Wellness Partnership Deliver

health partnership, and wearables

Design Ethical Intelligence

conversations into care navigation
and routing. Deliver Zero-incident

Lead AI thought leadership
Deliver AI guardrails for mental
health, research science studies on

innovation powers human care.
Deliver Measurable Outcomes

across subclinical wellness,
coaching, and clinical oﬀerings.

tracking, and Evidence-based care
models.
Scale B2B Benefit Management

employer and health plans. Deliver
ROI and impact dashboards.

Defining Currency

not just capability

care at Every Step
What isnʼt measurable
doesnʼt count
